title: For warning signs of the next global financial crisis, watch the activities of both banks and nonbanks
gist: The next financial crisis will be triggered by the same two fundamental factors as always—unstable funding and excess leverage—and watching specifically hedge funds (repo-dependent basis traders at $5.25T AUM) and banks' held-to-maturity accounting surprises is more predictive than the simplistic 'banks vs. NBFIs' framing dominating current G30 and IMF debate.

insights.claim: The G30 and IMF framing that financial stability risk has migrated from banks to nonbank financial institutions (NBFIs) is 'simplistic' and misleading—the correct analytical frame is identifying specific entities and activities vulnerable to unstable funding and high leverage, regardless of sector label.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Regulators and investors using 'NBFI' as a risk proxy are misfocusing; the actionable screen is for funding instability and leverage, not entity type.
insights.quote: Repeating the simplistic statement that the next global financial crisis will be due to the shift of lending from banks to NBFIs could mislead market participants, diverting attention from fundamental risk factors.
insights.locator: In short, in assessing current risks

insights.claim: Pension funds and insurance companies account for more than 30% of the NBFI sector's $260 trillion+ in assets—more than half of total global financial assets—and have stable long-term funding bases, making their growth a net stabilizer of the financial system, not a risk source.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Growth of pension/insurance AUM is routinely mislabeled as rising NBFI risk; the opposite is true—it enhances system stability, absent leveraged derivative overlays.
insights.quote: growth in these industries relative to other types of financial institutions tends to enhance the stability of the financial system
insights.locator: According to the G30 report, more than 30 percent

themes.theme: Hedge funds as the primary systemic risk vector

insights.claim: Hedge funds, with an estimated $5.25 trillion in AUM, have become dependent on the overnight repo market to fund highly leveraged basis and arbitrage positions—making them the primary NBFI source of systemic instability.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Overnight repo rates and hedge fund positioning are the leading indicators of systemic stress; a sudden tightening in repo conditions is the likely tripwire for the next dislocation.
insights.quote: hedge funds—whose assets under management are estimated to have reached $5.25 trillion—have become dependent on the overnight repo market to fund their highly leveraged investment positions
insights.locator: On the other hand, hedge funds

insights.claim: Several recent episodes of US Treasury market turmoil were triggered by hedge funds unwinding cash-vs-derivatives (basis trade) positions, demonstrating that hedge fund deleveraging can destabilize the world's most important sovereign bond market.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Monitor positioning data in Treasury cash-futures basis for signs of crowding; these are the most likely trigger for the next market dislocation and Fed emergency intervention.
insights.quote: several recent episodes of market turmoil were triggered by hedge funds unwinding their cash versus derivatives positions in the US Treasury security market
insights.locator: Specifically, many hedge funds have carried out arbitrage

insights.claim: Contagion risk has grown because money market mutual funds, banks, and principal trading companies are net lenders to hedge funds in the repo market—meaning hedge fund losses can propagate through the entire repo ecosystem simultaneously.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: The repo ecosystem creates unexpected contagion pathways: a hedge fund blow-up hits money market funds and banks simultaneously, compressing liquidity across asset classes.
insights.quote: contagion—or interconnectivity—risk has increased with the participation of institutions (including money market mutual funds, banks, and principal trading companies) in the repo market, becoming net lenders to hedge funds

insights.claim: Banks' held-to-maturity accounting—no mark-to-market requirement—creates 'surprise risk': losses are hidden until a bank is forced to sell at market prices, as demonstrated by Silicon Valley Bank's failure in 2023.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Banks holding large HTM portfolios in rising rate environments carry unreported losses; a liquidity squeeze forcing asset sales can trigger sudden loss recognition and contagious sell-offs.
insights.quote: when they need to sell these assets at market prices, realizing losses, this could come as a surprise to market participants, triggering reactive sell-offs that could become contagious
insights.locator: banks are not required to conduct mark-to-market

insights.claim: Stable-funding entities like UK pension funds can still create systemic risk if they use leveraged derivatives—demonstrated in the 2022 gilt crisis, where liability-driven investment (LDI) strategies triggered emergency margin calls and forced selling that nearly collapsed the gilt market.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Pension/insurance AUM is not inherently safe if subsidiaries or LDI strategies carry leverage; audit derivative exposure within ostensibly 'stable' entities, particularly where rates have moved sharply.
insights.quote: UK pension funds, which relied on gilt derivatives for their liability-driven investment strategies and suffered major losses in the 2022 gilt market crisis
